text: The phonological loop — one of Baddeley's working memory subsystems — stores verbal information through inner rehearsal, the silent repetition we perform when memorizing a phone number. Its capacity isn't measured in items but in time: roughly two seconds of speech. This explains why shorter words are remembered more easily than longer ones, a phenomenon called the word-length effect. It also explains why Chinese speakers outperform English speakers on digit span tasks — Mandarin number words are shorter and fit more easily within the loop's two-second window. Capacity, here, is not intelligence; it is phonology.
question: What determines the capacity of the phonological loop, according to the passage?

options.text: The number of syllables a person has been trained to recognize
The duration of speech that can be rehearsed in roughly two seconds
The number of items the central executive can allocate to rehearsal
The familiarity of the words being held in memory
